ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to cannelloni with ricotta and spinach; A00GJ (filled pasta, baked) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cannelloni as a specific food concept; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of cannelloni with ricotta and spinach weighs approximately 400 g, typically comprising 4–6 filled pasta tubes topped with bechamel sauce and baked until golden.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cannelloni, with ricotta and spinach filling, baked (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for cannelloni with ricotta, spinach, and bechamel as a composite dish. Values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked pasta (FDC ID 168936), part-skim ricotta cheese (FDC ID 173420), cooked spinach (FDC ID 170091), and bechamel sauce components (whole milk, butter, flour), cross-referenced against the input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) which are consistent with the composite estimate. Input values retained and validated against USDA component data. Confidence set to medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — pasta (filled, baked) analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cannelloni with ricotta and spinach. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for filled pasta / pasta dishes with protein and fat enrichment (GI range 35–45 for egg-enriched and filled pasta). The protein from ricotta (7.5 g/100g) and fat from bechamel and cheese (9.5 g/100g) slow gastric emptying and blunt the glycaemic response of the pasta carbohydrate. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 48.0) / 100 = 19.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 237.5 mg/100g; saturated fat 5.5 g/100g at cardiovascular threshold
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to 200 g to keep sodium intake below 475 mg per meal; request reduced added salt and Parmesan, and substitute butter-based bechamel with a lighter milk-only sauce to reduce both sodium and saturated fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 12 g/100g from refined pasta; low GI (~40) due to protein and fat co-ingestion
type2_diabetes.guidance: Limit serving to 200 g (approximately 24 g carbohydrate) and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to moderate postprandial glucose; the low GI of filled pasta is advantageous but refined flour in both pasta and bechamel requires portion discipline.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.5 g/100g at the upper threshold; cholesterol 30 mg/100g; trans fat 0.13 g/100g present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Limit portion to 200 g per meal to keep saturated fat below 11 g; choose a bechamel made with skimmed milk and minimal butter to reduce saturated and trans fat, and avoid additional Parmesan topping to manage LDL-raising fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 5.5 g/100g at pro-inflammatory threshold; spinach provides anti-inflammatory antioxidants
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Limit portion to 200 g and substitute butter-based bechamel with an olive-oil-based white sauce to reduce saturated fat and shift the fatty acid profile toward anti-inflammatory monounsaturated fat; spinach filling provides beneficial vitamin K and folate.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cannelloni with Ricotta and Spinach is a classic Italian baked pasta dish consisting of large cylindrical pasta tubes (cannelloni) filled with a creamy mixture of part-skim ricotta cheese and wilted spinach, seasoned with nutmeg, salt, pepper, and sometimes egg to bind the filling. The filled tubes are arranged in a baking dish, covered with a rich bechamel (white) sauce made from butter, flour, and whole milk, topped with grated Parmesan cheese, and baked in the oven at approximately 180–200°C until the surface is golden and bubbling. The dish has a soft, yielding texture from the pasta and filling, a creamy and mildly savoury flavour profile, and a lightly browned, cheesy crust. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 162.5 kcal, 7.5 g protein, 9.5 g fat (5.5 g saturated), 12 g carbohydrate, 1.5 g sugar, 1.0 g dietary fibre, 237.5 mg sodium, 112.5 mg calcium, and 0.88 mg iron. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low) due to the protein and fat content slowing gastric emptying. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension (borderline sodium), type 2 diabetes (moderate refined carbohydrate, mitigated by low GI), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat at threshold). It is suitable for vegetarians and is a good source of calcium and iron from the dairy and spinach components. A standard restaurant serving is approximately 400 g (one plate), providing around 650 kcal and 48 g carbohydrate.
common_user_queries: Baked pasta with ricotta and spinach nutrition

confidence_reason: Input record is ai_generated with confidence 0.7 from a delivery platform (grabfood), which is a forbidden primary nutrient source. Nutrient values have been cross-validated against a USDA FoodData Central composite estimate of the component ingredients (cooked pasta, part-skim ricotta, cooked spinach, bechamel components) and found to be consistent and plausible. Confidence is set to medium because no single approved-source entry for this exact composite dish exists; values are adapted from component-level USDA data.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite baked pasta dish is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16; component-level USDA data would require weighted fatty acid modelling beyond the scope of this record. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this composite dish is not reported as a single entry in the approved sources consulted; estimated ~180 mg/100g from spinach and dairy components but not confirmed by a single approved-source entry and therefore set to -1 per numeric hygiene rules. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this specific composite dish are not available as a validated single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. trans_fat_g is 0.13 g/100g, a small but non-zero value consistent with dairy-derived trans fats (vaccenic acid) from butter and cheese in the bechamel and Parmesan topping. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for filled and enriched pasta; no direct measurement for cannelloni with ricotta and spinach was found. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was treated as a menu listing reference only and not as a primary nutrient source per Project Veritas source policy. Nutrient values from the input record were retained after cross-validation against USDA component data confirmed plausibility. The dish is served in Singapore Italian restaurants and is listed on GrabFood Singapore; country_of_origin in the input was Singapore (delivery context) but cuisine origin is Italy.
